theme: Reverse-and-slice bug silences recent events, ghost naming collision breeds future haunting, seven reviews deep on the tagline ceremony — the debt is starting to echo

fixed_since_last_review.title: Worker confetti color regression — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: App.jsx:39-41 comment confirms WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES removed. App.jsx:1182 — workerConfetti container div now carries `style={{ '--worker-color': workerConfetti.color }}`. Color identity for each worker's shipping moment is restored.

fixed_since_last_review.title: Conference table last-huddle label at 0.38rem — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: Office.jsx:1001 — `style={{ opacity: 0.65, fontSize: '0.6rem', ... }}`. Both the font-size floor and the opacity target from the review were applied exactly.

fixed_since_last_review.title: isNight dead variable — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: Office.jsx:873-876 — the `isNight` declaration is gone. WindowElement now uses only `isMidnight`, `isDaytime`, and `isGoldenHour`. One less landmine for future contributors.

fixed_since_last_review.title: isStandup dead object-vs-string OR clause — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: Office.jsx:1443 — `const isStandup = occ && standupWorkers.some(sw => sw.id === occ.worker.id)`. The dead `|| sw.id === occ.worker` clause that compared a string to an object is gone.

fixed_since_last_review.title: idleWorkers permanently-true filter — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: Office.jsx:1218 — `nonMgr.filter(w => w.status === 'idle')`. The `!workingIds.has(w.id)` guard that was always true for idle workers has been removed. Intent is now unmistakable.

fixed_since_last_review.title: Activity log filter logic duplicated in two places — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: App.jsx:753-759 — `filteredActivityEntries` useMemo is the single source of truth. It feeds both the count label (line 1376) and the ActivityLog entries prop (line 1424). No drift possible.

fixed_since_last_review.title: WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES identical to CONFETTI_PIECES — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: App.jsx:39-41 — WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES removed. Worker confetti now reuses CONFETTI_PIECES directly (App.jsx:1182-1184) with the worker color applied via CSS custom property on the container div. Dead inventory eliminated.

ideas.title: ActivityLog `reverse().slice(20)` Shows the 20 Oldest Events, Not the 20 Newest
ideas.description: App.jsx:137 inside ActivityLog — `const displayed = [...entries].reverse().slice(0, 20)`. The `entries` prop is `filteredActivityEntries` which inherits the order of `activityLog`. The log is newest-first: synthetic vibe entries are always prepended (`[newEntry, ...prev].slice(0, 50)` at App.jsx:1098-1104), so `activityLog[0]` is always the most recent event. Reversing gives oldest-first (50 entries), then `slice(0, 20)` takes entries 31-50 in original ordering — the 20 OLDEST entries. The dashboard header says `'Latest ${DISPLAY_CAP} of ${N} events'` (App.jsx:1377), but when N > 20, the log shows the 20 stalest events. Users see yesterday's hires and not today's errors. Fix: swap the order — `entries.slice(0, 20).reverse()`. This takes the 20 most recent entries first, then reverses them to display oldest-of-those-20 first, preserving the chronological grouping ('Just now' → 'Earlier today' → 'Yesterday') that the ActivityLog component expects. WHY: this is a correctness bug that silently hides recent activity behind a label that promises the opposite. When the log is full (common during active shifts), users see a feed that looks populated but contains no data from the last 30+ minutes.

ideas.title: Ghost (Stale) Desk Gets `desk--active` Class and Lit Lamp Simultaneously with `desk--ghost`
ideas.description: Office.jsx:1447 and 1457 — When `isGhostDesk` is true (working worker, `updated_at` > 15 minutes ago), two conflicting visual states are applied at once. Line 1447: `${isWorking && !isStandup ? ' desk--active' : ''}${isGhostDesk ? ' desk--ghost' : ''}` — since `isGhostDesk` requires `isWorking === true`, both `desk--active` and `desk--ghost` are present in the className for any non-standup stale desk. Line 1457: `desk-lamp${isWorking ? ' desk-lamp--working' : ...}` — the lamp still glows with full role color when `isGhostDesk` is true. The intent of the ghost desk visual (dim, haunted, clearly 'something's off') directly contradicts the active desk visual (lit lamp, glowing border, role color). The two CSS classes fight each other and the result depends on specificity order. Fix: exclude `desk--active` and `desk-lamp--working` when `isGhostDesk` is true: `${isWorking && !isStandup && !isGhostDesk ? ' desk--active' : ''}` at line 1447; `isWorking && !isGhostDesk ? ' desk-lamp--working'` at line 1457. WHY: the ghost desk tier ('fresh' / 'stale' / 'abandoned') is meant to communicate 'this worker may have stepped away from keyboard'. A fully lit, role-colored, actively-glowing desk undermines that story completely.

ideas.title: `isGhostDesk` and `occ.ghost` — Two Unrelated 'Ghost' Concepts Sharing a Word
ideas.description: Office.jsx:1423-1447 — Two completely different concepts both use the word 'ghost': (1) `occ.ghost` (line 1314-1322) means the worker is a roster-only OOO member — they're not active at all, just a placeholder at a dark desk. (2) `isGhostDesk` (line 1429) means the worker IS working (`status === 'working'`), but their `updated_at` is more than 15 minutes stale — they're online but possibly AFK. These are not related. `isGhostDesk` is never true when `occ.ghost` is true (because `isGhostDesk` requires `isWorking`, which requires `!occ.ghost`). But a new contributor reading `isGhostDesk ? ' desk--ghost'` at line 1447 will naturally pattern-match on `occ.ghost` and assume the condition fires for OOO workers. It does not. This is a trap. Fix: rename `isGhostDesk` → `isStaleWorking` (or `isStaleDeskWorker`), rename the CSS class applied from `desk--ghost` to `desk--stale` (for the stale-working case), and keep `occ.ghost` for OOO. The two concepts live 8 lines apart and will only get more confusing as the desk rendering grows. WHY: naming is documentation. When two variables share a root word, readers assume they're related.

ideas.title: Ghost Desk `diffMs` Recomputed Three Times in the Same Render Tick
ideas.description: Office.jsx:1430, 1432, 1439 — `Date.now() - new Date(occ.worker.updated_at).getTime()` is evaluated three separate times for the same desk occupant in the same DESKS.map() iteration: once inline at line 1430 as part of `isGhostDesk`, once inside the IIFE at line 1432 (stored as `diff`), and once at line 1439 (stored as `diffMs`). Each call allocates a new `Date` object and invokes `Date.now()`. The three values will differ by microseconds (harmless), but the code is 13 lines that could be 5. The separate variable names `diff` and `diffMs` for the same computation reinforce the confusion. Fix: move the common computation before all three uses: `const ghostDiffMs = occ && occ.ghost === false && occ.worker.updated_at ? Date.now() - new Date(occ.worker.updated_at).getTime() : 0`, then replace all three inline computations with `ghostDiffMs`. WHY: three copies of the same computation is a classic maintainability hazard. If `updated_at` ever gains a timezone suffix or format change, three sites need updating instead of one.

ideas.title: Working Count Filter Called Twice Inline in the Same JSX Expression
ideas.description: App.jsx:1307-1309 — Inside the Active Workers section header, the same array filter runs twice in the same conditional expression: `activeWorkers.filter((w) => w.status === 'working').length > 0 ? \`🔥 ${activeWorkers.filter((w) => w.status === 'working').length} grinding hard\` : '😴 Everyone is on standby'`. The first call allocates an array to check `.length > 0`. The second call allocates a second identical array to read `.length` for the string. In the case where the result is falsy, only the first runs; in the truthy case, both run. Fix: `const displayWorkingCount = activeWorkers.filter(w => w.status === 'working').length` placed immediately before the `<div className='section-header'>` at line 1303. Use `displayWorkingCount` in both spots. This also makes the display logic easier to test in isolation. WHY: `activeWorkers` can have up to 6 entries, so this isn't a performance emergency. But the double filter signals to a reader that the two lengths might somehow differ — they can't, but the duplication implies they might.

ideas.title: ActivityLogEntry Key Uses Delimiter-Free String Concatenation
ideas.description: App.jsx:154 — `key={entry.timestamp + entry.worker + i}`. Concatenating strings without separators can produce colliding keys. Example: `timestamp='2026-03-11T05:00a'`, `worker='ya'`, `i=1` produces the same key `'2026-03-11T05:00aya1'` as `timestamp='2026-03-11T05:00'`, `worker='aya'`, `i=1`. Worker names and timestamps are controlled, so a collision has never occurred in production. But the key is a compound of three fields and the concatenation implies they're a single string. Fix: `` key={`${entry.timestamp}_${entry.worker ?? 'system'}_${i}`} `` — underscores as separators guarantee uniqueness since neither timestamps nor worker names contain underscores. WHY: React key collisions cause silent DOM reconciliation bugs (wrong component reuse, animation state leaks). The fix is two characters per separator.

ideas.title: quoteBonus Wrap Still Has No Ceremony — Seventh Consecutive Review
ideas.description: App.jsx:1437 — `p >= BONUS_TAGLINES.length ? 1 : p + 1` wraps silently. The `{quoteBonus > 0 && quoteBonus < BONUS_TAGLINES.length && ...}` counter disappears at `quoteBonus === 8` with no acknowledgment. The tagline hunt has been unfinished since review #83. Seven reviews. The ceremony pattern is fully in the codebase: localStorage flags (`footer-sparkle-seen` at line 751), completion toasts (line 1186-1209), golden border completions. This is assembly of existing parts. Fix: on `p >= BONUS_TAGLINES.length`, write `'footer-all-taglines-seen'` to localStorage. On the reset tick, fire a completion toast with golden border (`'#fbbf24'` color): `text: '✦ You found all of Bloberto's thoughts. All eight of them.'`. After the flag is set, render the ✦ sparkle permanently gold via a new `footer-sparkle--complete` class (no twinkle, steady glow). WHY: seventh consecutive review. The tagline hunt is the only active discovery mechanic in the app. A counter that goes `7/8 → gone` with no payoff is an anticlimactic ending to the only Easter egg in the footer.

ideas.title: 'Burning the Midnight Oil' Greeting Fires at 8pm
ideas.description: App.jsx:1160-1163 — `const isAfterHours = currentHour < 5 || currentHour >= 20`. The greeting `'🌃 Burning the midnight oil'` fires at hour 20 (8:00pm). The previous branch `'🌙 Good evening'` covers hours 18-19 (6-7pm). At 8pm on a Wednesday, most users are at dinner or winding down, not burning midnight oil. The phrase implies past midnight or at least 11pm+ hours. The `WallClock`'s own gradient treats hour 20 as 'night' sky (deep purple), not 'midnight' — reinforcing that 8pm is not midnight. Fix: `currentHour < 5 || currentHour >= 22` — reserve 'midnight oil' for 10pm–4am, and extend 'good evening' to cover 18-21. WHY: small copy detail, but the greeting is the first thing the user reads on mount. 'Burning the midnight oil at 8pm' registers as a minor off-note that slightly undermines the app's ambient authenticity.

ideas.title: `fadingMap ?? {}` Null-Guard Is a Misleading Signal
ideas.description: App.jsx:969 — `for (const w of [...activeWorkers, ...Object.values(fadingMap ?? {})]) {`. `fadingMap` is initialized with `useState({})` at line 743 — it is always a plain object, never null or undefined. The `?? {}` guard implies to a reader that `fadingMap` can be nullish and that the code defensively handles that case. It can't and doesn't. The same false guard appears nowhere else for other `useState({})` values (like `fadingTimersRef`). Fix: remove `?? {}` — `Object.values(fadingMap)` is correct and honest. WHY: defensive code that can never fire erodes the signal value of defensive code that can. When a reader sees `?? {}`, they allocate mental effort to understand when `fadingMap` could be null. The answer is never — that effort is wasted, and the code smell accumulates.

ideas.title: Office 'Deep Work' Ambient Badge: Visual Whisper for the Heads-Down Worker
ideas.description:
Wild idea: when a working worker has been on the same task for 45+ minutes (`updated_at` > 45 min ago — already detected at Office.jsx:836-838 for the hover card), render a tiny persistent ambient indicator directly on the Character in the office floor view. The hover card already shows '🎧 deep work' but this is invisible without a hover. Proposed: add a `
🎧
` rendered alongside `char__error-badge` (Office.jsx:804-806) — same size (8px × 8px), positioned top-left instead of top-right, with `opacity: 0.55` and a 4-second breathing pulse animation (scale 0.9 → 1.0 → 0.9, no color flash). No click handler. No tooltip. Just a quiet visual that says 'this person is in it.' The badge disappears automatically when the worker transitions to idle or their `updated_at` refreshes. No new props needed — the Character component already receives `worker.updated_at` and `worker.startedAt`. The detection threshold should match the hover card's existing 45-minute check for consistency. WHY: the office floor is the most expressive surface in the app. A typing character at a glowing desk tells the reader 'this person is working.' A headphone badge tells them 'this person should not be interrupted.' That's a qualitatively different story — and one that a real office reads at a glance but Bloberto's currently cannot.

_still_open_from_89.note: Seventh consecutive review. Escalating from 'should' to 'this has been on the board longer than three sprints.' Assigned to luna.
